setting.note: Walcott's book-length epic recasts Homer among the fishermen of St Lucia: Achille and Hector, who quarrel over the beautiful Helen; the wounded Philoctete; and the blind Seven Seas, the island's Omeros. Across the real island, from the fishing village of Gros Islet and the sea the men work, to the capital at Castries and the volcanic Pitons, the poem weaves the Aegean and the Caribbean, slavery's Middle Passage and the everyday life of a small Antillean nation into one great song of the sea.

rights.originalWork: In copyright. Derek Walcott, first published 1990. Mapped as a cited work: no text stored, no passages reproduced. The places are facts and the notes are our own.
rights.textSource: None stored. A cited book reproduces no text.
rights.territoryNotes: Locations and original commentary only. No reproduction of the copyrighted text, in any territory.
rights.attribution: Omeros, Derek Walcott (1990). Locations mapped by Read the Places; not affiliated with or endorsed by the author's estate or publisher.

palette.note: The wine-dark blue of the sea that is the poem's true subject, the Homeric Aegean and the Caribbean become one water. A cited book's colour is not a quotation, because we store no text to quote; it still has to be legible, and this clears WCAG AA on paper.
